verses.text: Now these were the sons of David, which were born unto him in Hebron; the firstborn Amnon, of Ahinoam the Jezreelitess; the second Daniel, of Abigail the Carmelitess: The third, Absalom the son of Maachah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur: the fourth, Adonijah the son of Haggith: The fifth, Shephatiah of Abital: the sixth, Ithream by Eglah his wife. These six were born unto him in Hebron; and there he reigned seven years and six months: and in Jerusalem he reigned thirty and three years. Guidance: The Chronicler names the six sons born to David during his seven-and-a-half years reigning in Hebron — Amnon, Daniel (called Chileab in 2 Samuel), Absalom, Adonijah, Shephatiah, and Ithream — each by a different mother. For the reader who knows David's story, these names carry the ache of a divided house: Amnon would commit a terrible crime against his sister and be murdered for it; Absalom would rebel against his father and die in revolt; Adonijah would grasp at the throne and likewise perish. The chapter is showing us, in the bare list of David's Hebron sons, the seedbed of the heartbreak that filled the king's later years — the bitter harvest, in his own household, of his sin and of the many wives he took against the LORD's word for a king (Deut 17:17). The chapter is teaching us that even the man after God's own heart reaped sorrow where he sowed disorder; a polygamous, fractured family bred rivalry, violence, and grief. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's covenant with David stood firm not because David's house was peaceful or pure, but because the LORD is faithful in spite of His servants' failures. The list of mothers and sons is a quiet sermon on the costliness of disordered desire, even in the life of a great believer — and a reminder that the everlasting throne promised to David would not rest on the merits of his sons but on the grace and purpose of God.

And these were born unto him in Jerusalem; Shimea, and Shobab, and Nathan, and Solomon, four, of Bathshua the daughter of Ammiel: Ibhar also, and Elishama, and Eliphelet, And Nogah, and Nepheg, and Japhia, And Elishama, and Eliada, and Eliphelet, nine. These were all the sons of David, beside the sons of the concubines, and Tamar their sister. Guidance: The sons born to David in Jerusalem are named, and here the genealogy quietly carries one of the most astonishing testimonies of grace in all of Scripture: 'Shimea, and Shobab, and Nathan, and Solomon... of Bathshua the daughter of Ammiel' — that is, of Bathsheba, the very woman David took in his great sin, the wife of Uriah whom he had killed. Yet from this marriage, born out of a story of adultery, murder, and repentance, came Solomon, the heir of the throne and builder of the temple — and Nathan, through whose line, Luke's Gospel tells us, the Messiah would also descend (Luke 3:31). The chapter is showing us the LORD's redeeming grace transforming the place of David's deepest shame into the channel of His highest blessing: the son of Bathsheba inherits the kingdom. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's mercy reaches into our worst failures and brings forth, not merely forgiveness, but fruitfulness — He took a union that began in scandal and, after true repentance (Psalm 51), made it the line of Solomon and of Christ. The chapter is teaching the truth that 'where sin abounded, grace did much more abound' (Rom 5:20). The mention of 'Tamar their sister' — the daughter wronged by Amnon — keeps the family's sorrows in view even here, but the dominant note is grace: that the LORD can write redemption into a history stained by sin, and bring the Savior of the world through a line marked at its heart by mercy to a repentant sinner.

And Solomon's son was Rehoboam, Abia his son, Asa his son, Jehoshaphat his son, Joram his son, Ahaziah his son, Joash his son, Amaziah his son, Azariah his son, Jotham his son, Ahaz his son, Hezekiah his son, Manasseh his son, Amon his son, Josiah his son. Guidance: Here the Chronicler races down the royal line of Judah — the unbroken succession of David's heirs on the throne in Jerusalem, from Solomon through Rehoboam, Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, Josiah, and the rest. This is the line that the books of Kings and Chronicles have chronicled at length: some of these kings walked with the LORD, many did not, yet the throne passed from father to son in a single dynasty for over three hundred years. The chapter is showing us the visible outworking of the LORD's covenant with David — 'thine house and thy kingdom shall be established for ever before thee: thy throne shall be established for ever' (2 Sam 7:16). Through good kings and evil ones, through reformation and apostasy, the LORD kept a lamp burning for David in Jerusalem (1 Kings 11:36; 15:4). The chapter is teaching us the faithfulness of God to His promise: not one of these kings deserved the throne by his own righteousness, and several came perilously close to extinguishing the line, yet the LORD preserved the Davidic dynasty unbroken because He had sworn to do so. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community — reading this list when there was no longer a king on the throne — that the dynasty which had endured so long had not been a human accident but a divine commitment; and that the same God who kept the line through every threat in the days of the monarchy would keep His promise still, until the true and final Son of David should come to take the throne forever. This is the genealogy of the kings, and beneath it runs the unbreakable thread of covenant grace.

And the sons of Josiah were, the firstborn Johanan, the second Jehoiakim, the third Zedekiah, the fourth Shallum. And the sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah his son, Zedekiah his son. Guidance: With the sons of Josiah, the genealogy reaches the tragic end of the kingdom. After good king Josiah fell at Megiddo, his sons and grandson reigned in quick, doomed succession — Jehoahaz (Shallum), Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin (Jeconiah), and Zedekiah — and under them Jerusalem fell, the temple was burned, and Judah went into Babylonian exile. The throne that had stood since David was cast down; the last kings were carried away captive or died in disgrace. The chapter is showing us the collapse of the monarchy, the dark hour when it seemed the Davidic promise itself had failed. Jeconiah in particular was placed under a fearful prophetic curse — 'Write ye this man childless... for no man of his seed shall prosper, sitting upon the throne of David' (Jer 22:30). The chapter is teaching us the sober reality of judgment: the LORD's covenant with David did not exempt the dynasty from the consequences of persistent rebellion, and the kingdom was brought down. Yet even here, the line did not die — Jeconiah's descendants are recorded in the very next verses. The chapter is teaching us that God's discipline, however severe, was not the end of His purpose; the throne fell, but the family of David survived the catastrophe, carried into exile but not erased. The genealogy stands as a testimony that even when God's promises seem buried under judgment, He preserves a remnant and a hope — for the line that went down into Babylon would come up again, and would one day bring forth the King whom no curse could bind and no exile could destroy.

And the sons of Josiah were, the firstborn Johanan, the second Jehoiakim, the third Zedekiah, the fourth Shallum. And the sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah his son, Zedekiah his son. And the sons of Jeconiah; Assir, Salathiel his son, Malchiram also, and Pedaiah, and Shenazar, Jecamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah. And the sons of Pedaiah were, Zerubbabel, and Shimei: and the sons of Zerubbabel; Meshullam, and Hananiah, and Shelomith their sister: And Hashubah, and Ohel, and Berechiah, and Hasadiah, Jushabhesed, five. And the sons of Hananiah; Pelatiah, and Jesaiah: the sons of Rephaiah, the sons of Arnan, the sons of Obadiah, the sons of Shechaniah. And the sons of Shechaniah; Shemaiah: and the sons of Shemaiah; Hattush, and Igeal, and Bariah, and Neariah, and Shaphat, six. And the sons of Neariah; Elioenai, and Hezekiah, and Azrikam, three. And the sons of Elioenai were, Hodaiah, and Eliashib, and Pelaiah, and Akkub, and Johanan, and Dalaiah, and Anani, seven. Guidance: This is the most precious passage in the whole genealogy for the Chronicler's first readers: the Davidic line continuing after the exile. From captive Jeconiah comes Salathiel (Shealtiel), and then Zerubbabel — the man who led the first return from Babylon and rebuilt the temple, the governor of post-exilic Judah, the very figure standing at the center of the restored community's hope (Hag 2:23, where the LORD calls Zerubbabel His 'signet,' reversing the curse on Jeconiah). The list runs on through Zerubbabel's children and grandchildren for several more generations, ending with Anani. The chapter is showing us the unbroken survival of David's house through the fire of judgment: the dynasty that fell with Zedekiah did not die: it lived on in exile and came home in Zerubbabel. The chapter is teaching the returned exiles — who had no king, who lived under foreign empire, whose temple was a shadow of Solomon's — that the line of David was alive, traceable, and still bearing the ancient promise. The throne stood empty, but the heir lived; the covenant was not annulled but only waiting. The chapter is teaching us that God's promises outlast every catastrophe: the same line that produced David and Solomon, that went down into Babylon, that returned in Zerubbabel, would continue (as Matthew's Gospel shows, naming Salathiel and Zerubbabel in Christ's own genealogy, Matt 1:12) until it brought forth Jesus the Messiah, the true King in whom David's throne is established forever. For a discouraged remnant, this list of names was a quiet, powerful word of hope: the story is not over; the LORD keeps His covenant to a thousand generations, and the Son of David is yet to come.

And the sons of Jeconiah; Assir, Salathiel his son, Malchiram also, and Pedaiah, and Shenazar, Jecamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah. And the sons of Pedaiah were, Zerubbabel, and Shimei: and the sons of Zerubbabel; Meshullam, and Hananiah, and Shelomith their sister: And Hashubah, and Ohel, and Berechiah, and Hasadiah, Jushabhesed, five. And the sons of Hananiah; Pelatiah, and Jesaiah: the sons of Rephaiah, the sons of Arnan, the sons of Obadiah, the sons of Shechaniah. And the sons of Shechaniah; Shemaiah: and the sons of Shemaiah; Hattush, and Igeal, and Bariah, and Neariah, and Shaphat, six. And the sons of Neariah; Elioenai, and Hezekiah, and Azrikam, three. And the sons of Elioenai were, Hodaiah, and Eliashib, and Pelaiah, and Akkub, and Johanan, and Dalaiah, and Anani, seven.
